From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

In Christian liturgy, the eighth day after a feast

"**Octave**" has two senses in [Christian liturgical](/wiki/Christian_liturgy "Christian liturgy") usage. In the first sense, it is the eighth day after a feast, [counted inclusively](/wiki/Inclusive_counting "Inclusive counting"), and so always falls on the same day of the week as the feast itself. The word is derived from [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *octava* (eighth), with *“dies*” (day) implied and understood. In the second sense, the term is applied to the whole eight-day period, during which certain major feasts came to be observed.[[1]](#cite_note-ODCC-1)

Octaves, not being successive, are quite distinct from [eight-day weeks](/wiki/Eight-day_week "Eight-day week") and simply refer to the return of the same day of a [seven-day week](/wiki/Seven-day_week "Seven-day week") in the [inclusive counting](/wiki/Inclusive_counting "Inclusive counting") system used in Latin (just as the ninth day was a return to the same day of a [nundinal cycle](/wiki/Nundinal_cycle "Nundinal cycle"), the eight-day week of the pre-Christian [Roman calendar](/wiki/Roman_calendar "Roman calendar")).

## Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Octave_(liturgy)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early history")]

The "eighth day" or *octava dies* was associated with the weekly Christian celebration of the resurrection of Christ every "eighth day", which became a name for [Sunday](/wiki/Sunday "Sunday").[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

As [circumcision](/wiki/Brit_milah "Brit milah") is performed in [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") on the “eighth day” after birth, the number [8](/wiki/8 "8") became associated in [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") with [Baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism"),[[4]](#cite_note-4) and [baptismal fonts](/wiki/Baptismal_font "Baptismal font") have from an early date often been [octagonal](/wiki/Octagon "Octagon").[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The practice of octaves was first introduced under [Constantine I](/wiki/Constantine_I "Constantine I"), when the dedication festivities of the basilicas at [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") and [Tyre, Lebanon](/wiki/Tyre,_Lebanon "Tyre, Lebanon") were observed for eight days. After these one-off occasions, annual liturgical feasts began to be dignified with an octave. The first such feasts accorded such were [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter"), [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost"), and in the [East](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity"), [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)").[[1]](#cite_note-ODCC-1) This occurred in the 4th century and served as a time for the newly converted to take a joyful retreat.[[8]](#cite_note-CE-8)

The development of octaves occurred slowly. From the 4th century to the 7th century, Christians observed octaves with a celebration on the eighth day, with little development of the liturgies within the intervening days. [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") was the next feast to receive an octave, and by the 8th century, Rome had developed liturgical octaves not only for Easter, Pentecost, and Christmas, but also for the [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)") as in the East, and the feast of the [dedication of a church](/wiki/Dedication_of_churches "Dedication of churches").[[8]](#cite_note-CE-8)

From the 7th century, saints’ feasts also began to have octaves (as an eighth-day feast, not eight days of feasting), among the oldest being those of [Saints Peter and Paul](/wiki/Feast_of_Saints_Peter_and_Paul "Feast of Saints Peter and Paul"), [Saint Lawrence](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence "Saint Lawrence") and [Saint Agnes](/wiki/Saint_Agnes "Saint Agnes"). From the 12th century, the custom arose of liturgical observance of the days between the first and the eighth day, as well as the eighth day.[[1]](#cite_note-ODCC-1) During the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), octaves for various other feasts and saints were celebrated, depending upon the [diocese](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese") or [religious order](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_religious_order "Roman Catholic religious order").[[8]](#cite_note-CE-8)

## Western Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Octave_(liturgy)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Western Christianity")]

### Catholic Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Octave_(liturgy)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Catholic Church")]

#### From Pius V to Pius XII

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Octave_(liturgy)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: From Pius V to Pius XII")]

While [Pope Pius V](/wiki/Pope_Pius_V "Pope Pius V") reduced the amount of octaves in 1568,[[1]](#cite_note-ODCC-1) these were still numerous. Not only on the eighth day from the feast but, with the exception of the octaves of Easter, Pentecost, and, to a lesser extent, Christmas, on all the intervening days the [liturgy](/wiki/Catholic_liturgy "Catholic liturgy") was the same as on the feast day itself, with the exact same prayers and [Scripture](/wiki/Sacred_Scripture "Sacred Scripture") readings.

Octaves were classified into several types. Easter and Pentecost had “specially privileged” octaves, during which no other feast whatsoever could be celebrated. Christmas, Epiphany, and [Corpus Christi](/wiki/Corpus_Christi_(feast) "Corpus Christi (feast)") had “privileged” octaves, during which certain highly ranked feasts might be celebrated. The octaves of other feasts allowed even more feasts to be celebrated therein.[[8]](#cite_note-CE-8)

To reduce the repetition of the same liturgy for several days, [Pope Leo XIII](/wiki/Pope_Leo_XIII "Pope Leo XIII") and [Pope Pius X](/wiki/Pope_Pius_X "Pope Pius X") made further distinctions, classifying octaves into three primary types: privileged, common, and simple. Privileged octaves were further arranged in a hierarchy of first, second, and third orders. For the first half of the 20th century, octaves were ranked in the following manner, which affected holding other celebrations within them:

* **Privileged Octaves**
  + ***Privileged Octaves of the First Order***
    - [Octave of Easter](/wiki/Octave_of_Easter "Octave of Easter")
    - Octave of [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost")
  + ***Privileged Octaves of the Second Order***
    - Octave of [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)")
    - Octave of [Corpus Christi](/wiki/Corpus_Christi_(feast) "Corpus Christi (feast)")
  + ***Privileged Octaves of the Third Order***
    - Octave of [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas")
    - Octave of the [Ascension](/wiki/Ascension_of_Jesus "Ascension of Jesus")
    - Octave of the [Sacred Heart](/wiki/Sacred_Heart "Sacred Heart")
* **Common Octaves**
  + Octave of the [Immaculate Conception BVM](/wiki/Immaculate_Conception "Immaculate Conception")
  + Octave of the [Solemnity of St. Joseph](/wiki/Saint_Joseph%27s_Day "Saint Joseph's Day")
  + Octave of the [Nativity of St. John the Baptist](/wiki/Nativity_of_St._John_the_Baptist "Nativity of St. John the Baptist")
  + Octave of [Ss. Peter and Paul](/wiki/Feast_of_Saints_Peter_and_Paul "Feast of Saints Peter and Paul")
  + Octave of the [Assumption BVM](/wiki/Assumption_of_Mary "Assumption of Mary")
  + Octave of [All Saints](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Day "All Saints' Day")
* **Simple Octaves**
  + Octave of [St. Stephen](/wiki/Saint_Stephen "Saint Stephen")
  + Octave of [St. John the Apostle](/wiki/John_the_Apostle "John the Apostle")
  + Octave of the [Holy Innocents](/wiki/Massacre_of_the_Innocents "Massacre of the Innocents")
  + Octave of [St. Lawrence](/wiki/Lawrence_of_Rome "Lawrence of Rome")
  + Octave of the [Nativity BVM](/wiki/Nativity_of_Mary "Nativity of Mary")

In addition to these, the [patron saint](/wiki/Patron_saint "Patron saint") of a particular nation, diocese, or church was celebrated therein with an octave, on each day of which the Mass and [Office](/wiki/Liturgy_of_the_Hours "Liturgy of the Hours") of the feast was repeated, unless impeded by a higher-ranked celebration.

Although the feasts of St. Lawrence and the [Nativity of the Blessed Virgin Mary](/wiki/Nativity_of_the_Blessed_Virgin_Mary "Nativity of the Blessed Virgin Mary") officially still had simple octaves, by the 20th century they had all but vanished as higher-ranking feasts were added to the calendar. The octave day alone of St. Lawrence was still commemorated during the Mass of [St. Hyacinth](/wiki/Hyacinth_of_Poland "Hyacinth of Poland"). The entire octave of the Nativity of the Blessed Virgin Mary was impeded, but [The Most Holy Name of Mary](/wiki/Holy_Name_of_Mary "Holy Name of Mary") was celebrated during the octave, and [The Seven Sorrows of the Blessed Virgin Mary](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Sorrows "Our Lady of Sorrows") was celebrated on the former octave day.

#### Reductions by Pius XII and Paul VI

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Octave_(liturgy)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Reductions by Pius XII and Paul VI")]

[Pope Pius XII](/wiki/Pope_Pius_XII "Pope Pius XII") further simplified the Calendar with a decree dated 23 March 1955: only the octaves of Christmas, Easter and Pentecost were kept, as these did not repeat the same liturgy daily. All other octaves in the [Roman Rite](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite") were suppressed, including those of local calendars (see [General Roman Calendar of Pope Pius XII#Octaves](/wiki/General_Roman_Calendar_of_Pope_Pius_XII#Octaves "General Roman Calendar of Pope Pius XII")). In 1969, the Church further revised the Calendar by deleting the Octave of Pentecost.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The first eight days of the Easter Season were made the Octave of Easter and celebrated as Solemnities of the Lord, with proper readings and prayers.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The [Second Sunday of Easter](/wiki/Second_Sunday_of_Easter "Second Sunday of Easter"), which ends the Easter Octave, has also been called “White Sunday” *(Dominica in albis),* among other traditional names*.*

The Christmas Octave is presently arranged as follows:

* Sunday within the octave: Feast of the [Holy Family](/wiki/Holy_Family "Holy Family"); celebrated on Friday, December 30 when Christmas falls on a Sunday
* 26 December: Feast of [Saint Stephen](/wiki/Saint_Stephen "Saint Stephen")
* 27 December: Feast of [John the Apostle](/wiki/John_the_Apostle "John the Apostle")
* 28 December: Feast of the [Holy Innocents](/wiki/Holy_Innocents "Holy Innocents")
* 29-31 December: days within the octave, with assigned readings and prayers, on which the celebration of optional memorials is permitted according to special rubrics (but as noted above, when Christmas is a Sunday, the Feast of the Holy Family is celebrated on December 30)
* 1 January, octave day of the Nativity; [Solemnity of Mary, Mother of God](/wiki/Solemnity_of_Mary,_Mother_of_God "Solemnity of Mary, Mother of God")[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Lutheran Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Octave_(liturgy)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Lutheran Church")]

Further information: [Liturgical calendar (Lutheran)](/wiki/Liturgical_calendar_(Lutheran) "Liturgical calendar (Lutheran)")

The [liturgical calendar](/wiki/Liturgical_calendar_(Lutheran) "Liturgical calendar (Lutheran)") of the [Lutheran Churches](/wiki/Lutheran_Church "Lutheran Church") may have octaves for the following feasts: "The Nativity of Our Lord, The Epiphany of Our Lord, The Resurrection of Our Lord, All Saints, Ascension Day, Pentecost and [Trinity Sunday](/wiki/Trinity_Sunday "Trinity Sunday")."[[12]](#cite_note-ALPB1934-12)

### Anglican Communion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Octave_(liturgy)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Anglican Communion")]

Churches within the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion") traditionally observed octaves associated with the feasts of [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas"), [Corpus Christi](/wiki/Feast_of_Corpus_Christi "Feast of Corpus Christi"), [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)"), [Michaelmas](/wiki/Michaelmas "Michaelmas"), [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter"), and [All Saints' Day](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Day "All Saints' Day").[[13]](#cite_note-13) Many provinces have followed the Catholic Church and altered the practice of observing octaves. In the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), the only octave that remains in some form is that of Easter: no other feasts may be celebrated in the six days following Easter Sunday, and only a Dedication Festival or Patronal Festival may be celebrated on the Second Sunday of Easter.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Eastern Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Octave_(liturgy)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Eastern Christianity")]

Main article: [Afterfeast](/wiki/Afterfeast "Afterfeast")

Among the Eastern Orthodox and [Byzantine Rite](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite "Byzantine Rite") [Eastern Catholic Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches"), what in the West would be called an Octave is referred to as an **Afterfeast**. The celebration of the [Great Feasts](/wiki/Great_Feasts_of_the_Orthodox_Church "Great Feasts of the Orthodox Church") of the [church year](/wiki/Church_year#Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Church year") are extended for a number days, depending upon the particular Feast. Each day of an Afterfeast will have particular hymns assigned to it, continuing the theme of the Feast being celebrated.

Most of these Great Feasts (except Feasts within the moveable Paschal Cycle) also have a day or more of preparation called a **Forefeast**. Forefeasts and Afterfeasts will affect the structure of the services during the [Canonical Hours](/wiki/Canonical_hours#Eastern_Orthodox_usage "Canonical hours").

The last day of an Afterfeast is called the ***Apodosis*** (lit. "giving-back") of the Feast. On the *Apodosis*, most of the hymns that were chanted on the first day of the Feast are repeated. On the Apodoses of Feasts of the [Theotokos](/wiki/Theotokos "Theotokos"), the Epistle and Gospel of the Feast are repeated again at the [Divine Liturgy](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy "Divine Liturgy").

## Non-liturgical usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Octave_(liturgy)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Non-liturgical usage")]

The term "octave" is applied to some church observances that are not strictly liturgical. For example, many churches observe an annual "Octave of Prayer for [Christian Unity](/wiki/Ecumenism "Ecumenism")", which runs from 18 January to 25 January. The octave was established in 1895 by [Pope Leo XIII](/wiki/Pope_Leo_XIII "Pope Leo XIII") for the period between [Ascension](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Ascension "Feast of the Ascension") and [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost"). In 1909, [Pope Pius X](/wiki/Pope_Pius_X "Pope Pius X") approved the transfer of this octave to the period between the former feast of the [Chair of St. Peter](/wiki/Chair_of_St._Peter "Chair of St. Peter") (then on January 18) and the feast of the [Conversion of St. Paul](/wiki/Conversion_of_St._Paul "Conversion of St. Paul") (January 25).[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In 1968, the [World Council of Churches](/wiki/World_Council_of_Churches "World Council of Churches") and the [Vatican](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See")'s [Pontifical Council for Promoting Christian Unity](/wiki/Pontifical_Council_for_Promoting_Christian_Unity "Pontifical Council for Promoting Christian Unity") agreed to jointly publish prayer materials for the occasion under the title "[Week of Prayer for Christian Unity](/wiki/Week_of_Prayer_for_Christian_Unity "Week of Prayer for Christian Unity")", but it is still often referred to as an octave, especially within the [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism"), [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") and [Anglo-Catholic](/wiki/Anglo-Catholic "Anglo-Catholic") traditions.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The Week of Prayer is observed at various times around the world, especially in the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere") where it is commonly observed from Ascension to Pentecost.

Each year, [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg") celebrates the [*Oktav*](/wiki/Octave_celebration "Octave celebration") in honour of [Our Lady of Luxembourg](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Luxembourg "Our Lady of Luxembourg"), patroness of the city. Despite its name, the occasion is held from the 3rd to the 5th Sunday after [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter"), making it 15 instead of 8 days.
